original_abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on instruction tuning—the process of finetuning on a diverse range of tasks—to align their behavior with human intent. However, human-curated instruction data is often limited in volume, lacking in diversity, or costly to produce. We introduce SELF-INSTRUCT, a semi-automated framework for improving the instruction-following capabilities of pretrained language models by leveraging their own generation power. Specifically, our pipeline uses a small set of seed human-written instructions to prompt the model to generate new tasks and corresponding input-output instances. By applying rigorous filtering to ensure quality and diversity, the model iteratively bootstraps its own training data. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SELF-INSTRUCT significantly enhances the performance of a vanilla GPT-3 model, narrowing the gap with state-of-the-art models that were trained on private, human-annotated datasets. We release our synthetic dataset of 52,000 instructions to facilitate further research into the self-alignment of large-scale models.
humanized_abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) suggest that instruction tuning—the process of fine-tuning a model on a broad set of tasks—is an important factor for aligning model output to human desires. However, the main bottleneck of instruction tuning is that high-quality human-written instructions for instructing LLMs are often relatively short in supply, due to their limited diversity, the efforts needed to acquire them, and their costly nature. In this paper, we present SELF-INSTRUCT a self-supervised method for improving the functionality of LLMs, while making efficient use of any minimal seed human-written instructions available. Our framework leverages the power of LLMs to generate novel examples given a given set of seed human-written instructions. We then develop an efficient filter to produce diverse samples in a large quantity. We then learn from the filtered samples in a self-supervised manner and repeatedly recycle the loop to generate more samples from the model itself. Experiments show that SELF-INSTRUCT vastly outperforms a vanilla GPT-3 model and is often close to the performance of LLMs that receive extensive private human-annotated instruction datasets. We make our SELF-INSTRUCT generated training data of 52K instructions available, which we believe will also facilitate future works on self-aligned large-scale LLMs.
